event: The Peaceable Kingdom — Isaiah's vision of harmony among creatures
symbols: lion lying with the calf — the cessation of predatory violence in God's redeemed creation
child among wild beasts — innocence reigning over the animal kingdom without fear
leopard with kid — natural enemies reconciled by divine peace
Penn's treaty in background — the human aspiration to live out Isaiah's peaceable vision
lush landscape — the restored Eden, creation healed
description: A landscape divided into foreground and background. In the foreground at right, a lion, a white ox, a leopard, and other wild and domestic animals rest together peacefully. Several small children sit or stand among them, one child's arm draped around the lion's neck. The animals are calm and docile. In the background at left, a group of figures — some in European dress, others appearing to be Native Americans — gather beneath trees near a river, seemingly engaged in a peace negotiation. The landscape features autumn-colored trees, a broad river, and distant blue hills.
theological_description: Edward Hicks's Peaceable Kingdom is a direct visual interpretation of Isaiah 11:6-9, one of the most beloved messianic prophecies: 'The wolf will live with the lamb, the leopard will lie down with the goat, the calf and the lion and the yearling together; and a little child will lead them.' Isaiah envisions a future age when the Messiah — the shoot from the stump of Jesse (Isaiah 11:1) — establishes a reign of such perfect justice and peace that even the natural order of predation is reversed. The painting literalizes this: lion, leopard, ox, and child coexist without fear or violence. In the background, Hicks places William Penn's treaty with the Lenape people, reflecting the Quaker conviction that Isaiah's peace is not merely eschatological but should be pursued in present human relations. The theological vision is ultimately one of cosmic restoration — what Romans 8:19-21 describes as creation itself being 'liberated from its bondage to decay.' The Peaceable Kingdom anticipates the new heavens and new earth (Isaiah 65:25, Revelation 21:1) where 'the lion will eat straw like the ox' and 'they will neither harm nor destroy on all my holy mountain.'

scripture_refs.reason: The wolf with the lamb, the leopard with the goat, the child leading wild animals — the exact prophecy illustrated
The messianic shoot from Jesse whose reign of justice produces the peaceable kingdom
The wolf and the lamb shall feed together and the lion shall eat straw — the eschatological restoration
Creation groaning for liberation from bondage to decay — the cosmic scope of redemption
The new heaven and new earth where there is no more death or mourning — the ultimate fulfillment
